title: Why Intuition Is Often Right Even When It Doesn't Make Sense
meta_description: Trust your gut even when it defies logic. Learn why intuition works and how to use it without needing proof.
og_title: Why Intuition Is Often Right Even When It Doesn't Make Sense
og_description: Trust your gut even when it defies logic. Learn why intuition works and how to use it without needing proof.
body:
You've Felt It Before

You know that moment. You meet someone new, and something in your gut says stay away. They seem perfectly nice. They smile. They say all the right things. But you feel a tightness in your chest, a cold wash of unease that you can't explain. Later, you find out they lied about something. Or they turned out to be manipulative. And you think, "I knew it."

That's your intuition. And it's not random. It's real. But here's the thing that drives most of us crazy: it almost never comes with a receipt. It doesn't hand you a list of evidence. It doesn't explain why. It just drops a feeling into your body and expects you to trust it. And when you're someone who thinks things through, who likes to make sense of the world, that's maddening. Right?!

You've been taught that being rational is the safe path. That decisions need reasons you can point to. That feelings are messy and unreliable. But what if I told you that your intuition is often right precisely because it doesn't make sense? That its power comes from its ability to bypass the logical mind and tap into something faster, deeper, and more accurate than you've been led to believe?

Let's get into it.

"Intuition is the intelligence of the nervous system. It doesn't need to explain itself because it already knows. Your job is to stop asking for permission to trust it."

Your Brain Is a Prediction Machine

Your brain is constantly running simulations. Every second, it's taking in millions of bits of information from your environment - the tone of someone's voice, the micro-expressions on their face, the posture of their body, the rhythm of their breathing, the temperature of the room. You don't consciously register most of this. It happens below the surface, in the parts of your brain that evolved long before you had language.

This is the same system that tells you a snake is dangerous before you've even fully seen it. It's the same system that makes you pull your hand back from a hot stove before the pain registers. It's survival machinery. And it's incredibly fast.

When your gut says "something's off" about a situation, what's really happening is that your brain has run a pattern match against everything it's ever experienced. It's detected a mismatch between what you're seeing and what it expects to see. The mismatch creates a feeling - tension, unease, a sense of wrongness. That feeling is information. It's not a guess. It's a conclusion your brain arrived at without your permission.

The problem is, your conscious mind wants to overrule it. You want to be fair. You don't want to judge someone based on a feeling. You want to give people the benefit of the doubt. And that's noble. But it's also how you end up ignoring the very signals that are trying to protect you.

Why Your Intuition Doesn't Come With an Explanation

This is the part that trips most people up. You want to know why you feel something. You want the reasoning. You want the data points. But your intuition doesn't work that way. It's not a lawyer presenting a case. It's a messenger that shows up with a single piece of paper that says "no" or "yes" or "wait" or "go."

Think about it like this: have you ever walked into a room and immediately felt the tension between two people who just had a fight? No one said anything. Nothing obvious was out of place. But you felt it. You could cut the air with a knife. That's your nervous system picking up on cues that your conscious mind missed - the slight shift in body language, the avoidance of eye contact, the tightness in someone's jaw.

Your intuition is constantly reading the room. It's reading the energy of the people around you. It's picking up on things you can't articulate. And it's giving you a summary report in the form of a feeling. That feeling is accurate. But it won't give you the footnotes.

If you wait for your intuition to explain itself, you'll be waiting forever. It's not built for that. It's built for speed. By the time your logical mind catches up and starts analyzing, the moment has passed. The decision point is gone.

Does that land? I hope so, because this is where most people get stuck. They want the intuitive hit to come with a detailed report. But it never will. You have to learn to trust the hit itself.

The Body Keeps the Score

Your intuition lives in your body. It's not a thought. It's a physical sensation. You feel it in your chest, your stomach, your throat, your shoulders. When something is right for you, your body relaxes. Your breathing deepens. There's a sense of ease. When something is wrong, you tighten up. You feel nauseous. Your shoulders creep up toward your ears. You get a headache.

These are not random. They are signals. And they are more reliable than your thoughts because your thoughts can be influenced by fear, hope, wishful thinking, and social pressure. Your body, on the other hand, is honest. It doesn't care what other people think. It doesn't care if you're being polite. It just tells you the truth.

If you've been told your whole life that you're "too sensitive" or that you "feel things too much," this is for you. You're not broken. Your nervous system is calibrated for a wider range than most people's. You pick up on things they miss. You feel the undercurrents. And that's not a weakness. It's a gift. But it's a gift that comes with a learning curve.

You have to learn to separate your own anxiety from genuine intuitive hits. Anxiety feels frantic, scattered, repetitive. It loops. It spins. It's loud. Intuition is quieter. It's a single note. It's a clear "no" or a clear "yes." It doesn't argue with itself. It doesn't need you to convince yourself. It just knows.

One of the best ways to start distinguishing between the two is to practice body scanning. Sit quietly, close your eyes, and ask yourself a yes-or-no question about something small. "Should I drink water right now?" or "Should I take a different route home?" Then feel what happens in your body. Does your chest open up? Does your stomach relax? That's a yes. Does your throat tighten? Does your gut clench? That's a no. Start with the small stuff. Build your trust muscles.

If you want to go deeper on this, I highly recommend checking out Sonia Choquette's Trust Your Vibes. She's been teaching this for decades, and her work is a great starting point for anyone who wants to get practical about developing their intuitive sensitivity.

Intuition Doesn't Care About Your Plans

One of the hardest lessons is that your intuition will often contradict what you want to be true. You want that relationship to work. You want that job. You want to move to that city. But your gut says no. And it's not because it's trying to ruin your life. It's because it sees something you don't want to see.

Your logical mind will fight it. It will come up with reasons why the feeling is wrong. "But they have so many good qualities." "But the pay is great." "But everyone says this is a good opportunity." And all of that might be true. But your intuition is looking at the bigger picture. It's factoring in things you can't consciously access - the long-term trajectory, the hidden costs, the energy drain, the misalignment with your soul's path.

I've seen this play out countless times. Someone gets a strong "no" about a relationship, but they ignore it because the person checks all the boxes on paper. Two years later, they're miserable. Or someone gets a strong "yes" about a business idea that seems crazy, but they follow it anyway, and it works out in ways they never could have predicted.

Intuition is not logical. It's not linear. It's not safe. But it's often right. And the more you ignore it, the louder it gets. It will keep showing up until you listen. And if you keep ignoring it, it will start showing up as physical symptoms - anxiety, fatigue, illness. Your body will force you to pay attention.

So here's the question: are you willing to be wrong in the short term to be right in the long term? Because that's what trusting your intuition often requires. You might look foolish. You might have to cancel plans. You might have to walk away from something that looks good on paper. But your soul knows the difference between what looks good and what is good.

The Research Backs This Up

This isn't just woo-woo nonsense. There's real science behind it. Researchers like Antonio Damasio have shown that people with damage to the part of the brain that processes emotional signals (the ventromedial prefrontal cortex) become terrible decision-makers. They can analyze options endlessly, but they can't decide. They lose the ability to feel their way through choices. Without the emotional signal, they're stuck.

This is called the somatic marker hypothesis. Your body marks experiences with physical sensations. These markers guide your decisions faster than conscious reasoning can. And they're often more accurate because they're based on accumulated experience, not just the data in front of you.

Another study by Ap Dijksterhuis showed that people who made complex decisions (like choosing a car or an apartment) without overthinking actually made better choices than people who analyzed every detail. The unconscious mind is better at integrating large amounts of information. It sees patterns the conscious mind misses.

So when your intuition tells you something, it's not a mystical voice from the ether. It's your own brain and body working together at a level of speed and complexity your conscious mind can't match. You're not being irrational. You're being super-rational. You're using a system that's faster and more complete than your logical analysis.

If you want to read more about this, Gary Klein's The Power of Intuition is a fantastic resource. He studied how firefighter commanders, nurses, and chess masters make split-second decisions using intuition. It's not guesswork. It's expertise that has become automatic.

How to Start Trusting Your Intuition

If you're reading this and thinking, "Okay, I'm on board, but how do I actually do this?" here's a practical starting point. You don't need to overhaul your entire life overnight. You just need to start small.

Step 1: Create space. Your intuition can't be heard if you're constantly distracted. You need quiet. You need to slow down. This might mean turning off the podcast on your commute. It might mean sitting in silence for five minutes before you make a decision. It might mean taking a walk without your phone. The noise of modern life drowns out the subtle signals. You have to create room for them.

Step 2: Ask and wait. When you have a decision to make, ask yourself "yes or no?" and then wait. Don't force an answer. Don't analyze. Just feel. The first sensation that comes up is usually the right one. Your logical mind will jump in quickly to argue, but the first hit is the intuitive one. Trust it.

Step 3: Test it on small things. Don't start with life-or-death decisions. Start with what to eat for lunch, which route to take, whether to call a friend or text. Notice what happens when you follow your gut on these small things. You'll start to build evidence that it works. And that evidence will make it easier to trust it on bigger things.

Step 4: Keep a log. Write down your intuitive hits and what happened. "I had a feeling I shouldn't go to that party. I stayed home and got a good night's sleep. The party was a disaster." This creates a track record. When your logical mind tries to dismiss your intuition, you can look back at the log and say, "No, actually, I have proof that I'm right most of the time."

If you want a more structured approach, Judith Orloff's Survival Guide for Highly Sensitive People is a great companion. She's a psychiatrist who specializes in helping sensitive people work through the world without losing their minds. She gets it.

What About When You're Wrong?

Let's be real. Your intuition will not be right 100% of the time. No one's is. You will sometimes get a strong feeling that turns out to be wrong. That's part of the process. It doesn't mean your intuition is broken. It means you're human.

The key is to figure out why it was wrong. Was it actually anxiety masquerading as intuition? Were you projecting a past experience onto a present situation? Were you tired, hungry, or stressed? Your body's signals can get scrambled when you're depleted. That's not a failure. That's information.

When you get it wrong, don't beat yourself up. Don't throw out the whole system. Just adjust. Ask yourself what you missed. Learn the difference between a clear intuitive hit and a fear-based reaction. The more you practice, the better you'll get at distinguishing them.

And here's the thing: even when your intuition is wrong, the act of trusting it builds something important. It builds self-trust. It builds your relationship with yourself. It teaches you that you're someone who listens to your own inner voice. And that is worth more than being right every single time.

Melissa Bond's Blood Orange Night is a memoir that touches on this theme of learning to trust your body's signals even when the world tells you you're wrong. It's a powerful read for anyone who's ever been gaslit into doubting their own perceptions.

Your Intuition Is a Relationship

Here's what I want you to walk away with. Your intuition is not a tool you use when you need an answer. It's a relationship you cultivate over time. It speaks to you through your body, your dreams, your sudden insights, your unexplainable feelings. The more you listen, the clearer it gets. The more you ignore it, the quieter it becomes. Until it stops speaking altogether.

You don't have to understand it to trust it. You don't have to explain it to follow it. You just have to be willing to look a little foolish, to go against the grain, to say "I don't know why, but I know."

And that's hard. I'm not going to pretend it's easy. It goes against everything you've been taught. But I promise you, the more you practice, the more you'll see that your intuition is not your enemy. It's not a random impulse. It's not a sign of weakness. It's your deepest wisdom speaking in the only language it has: feeling.

So the next time you get that hit, that unexplainable knowing, pause. Breathe. And before you dismiss it, ask yourself one question: "What if I'm right?"

What if your intuition is trying to save you time, pain, or heartache? What if it's trying to guide you toward something better than you could have imagined? What if the only thing standing between you and a life that fits you is your willingness to trust the thing that doesn't make sense?

You already know the answer. You've always known. You just needed permission to listen.

If you're ready to go deeper, take the intuition assessment to see where you're already strong and where you might be blocking your own knowing. It's a quick read, and it might show you something you've been missing.
